medications.indication_rationale: Depigmented skin has no melanin and burns readily; daily broad-spectrum sunscreen to the affected areas prevents sunburn and reduces the cosmetic contrast between depigmented and normal skin. This is the single management step the AI Doctor should actively recommend now.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply liberally to the depigmented areas each morning and reapply as needed with sun exposure

medications.indication_rationale: Applying a plain moisturiser soothes the reactive erythema from scrubbing and gives the patient a gentle, non-harmful action to replace the harmful scrubbing.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply a plain moisturiser to the area as needed

medications.indication_rationale: A recognised repigmentation option for facial vitiligo and generally preferred over potent steroids on facial skin — but it is an ELECTIVE treatment to be initiated by the GP or dermatologist at routine review, NOT started in this acute telehealth encounter. No dose is given here because none is defined by the source and it is not being prescribed now.
medications.deprescribing_note: Defer initiation to the routine dermatology/GP review; do not commence in this encounter.

medications.drug_name: Potent topical corticosteroid to facial skin
medications.indication_rationale: Starting a potent topical steroid on periorificial facial skin in an unconfirmed lesion via telehealth risks skin atrophy, telangiectasia and perioral dermatitis; any steroid choice for vitiligo is a specialist/GP decision at in-person review, not an acute telehealth prescription.

Empirically treating the lesion as a fungal infection (pityriasis versicolor) would be an error of commission driven by the patient's feared diagnosis — the depigmented, non-scaly character argues against it and it warrants confirmation, not blind antifungal treatment.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Stop all rubbing and scrubbing of the patch; apply a plain moisturiser instead.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: The peri-lesional redness is entirely caused by mechanical scrubbing; stopping it lets the erythema settle by morning, which itself demonstrates the skin is healthy.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Avoid repeated mirror-checking of the area for the rest of the night and use simple grounding/reassurance strategies.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces the anxiety-driven checking and scrubbing cycle and prevents an avoidable emergency-department presentation tonight.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Apply daily sunscreen to the depigmented skin going forward.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Amelanotic skin lacks natural photoprotection and burns easily.

patient_education_points.point: The white patch is a harmless loss of skin pigment. It is not an infection, it is not contagious, it does not cause pain, and the skin underneath is structurally healthy.
The redness around it is only there because the area was scrubbed hard in a panic — it will settle once the scrubbing stops.
Because the pale skin has no natural pigment, it burns easily in the sun, so daily sunscreen to that area is important.
This does not need an emergency department visit tonight; a routine GP or dermatology appointment is the right next step to confirm it and discuss treatment or camouflage options.
A GP can also check thyroid function, since this type of pigment loss is sometimes linked with autoimmune thyroid conditions.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: In-person confirmation of the diagnosis (examination +/- Wood's lamp), exclusion of a fungal/chemical mimic, discussion of repigmentation treatment and camouflage, thyroid screening, and the patient's ongoing anxiety.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: The patch breaking open, bleeding, becoming hot or painful, rapidly spreading, developing thick scale, or the patient's distress becoming unmanageable.

must_include_items: Recognise the finding as benign depigmentation and NOT an emergency
Provide clear reassurance about the painless, non-infective, structurally-intact nature of the skin
Explicitly acknowledge the telehealth limitation — a fungal/chemical mimic cannot be fully excluded remotely — and arrange routine in-person GP/dermatology confirmation
Instruct the patient to stop scrubbing the area and apply a plain moisturiser
Advise daily sunscreen to the depigmented skin
De-escalate the acute anxiety and prevent an unnecessary emergency-department presentation
acceptable_alternatives: Recommending a routine dermatology review directly rather than via the GP
Offering written/SMS reassurance and safety-netting as an adjunct
errors_of_omission: Failing to arrange any in-person confirmation (over-confident remote dismissal)
Failing to advise photoprotection of the depigmented skin
Failing to address the acute distress
errors_of_commission: Escalating to an emergency department or ambulance for a benign, painless, stable lesion
Empirically prescribing an antifungal for the feared infection
Starting a potent topical steroid on facial skin via telehealth
Diagnosing a 'flesh-eating infection' or necrosis and alarming the patient further
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES both correct de-escalation (no over-triage to ED/000) AND arranging routine in-person confirmation (no over-confident dismissal). Escalating this benign presentation to emergency care, or prescribing empiric antifungal/potent facial steroid, are the defining failures.
